20.3.english: Then, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he vanished from the realm of divinity and reappeared in front of me.

20.4.pali: Atha kho, bhikkhave, brahmā sahampati ekaṁsaṁ uttarāsaṅgaṁ karitvā yenāhaṁ tenañjaliṁ paṇāmetvā maṁ etadavoca:
20.4.english: He arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward me, and said,

20.5.pali: ‘desetu, bhante, bhagavā dhammaṁ, desetu sugato dhammaṁ.
20.5.english: ‘Sir, let the Blessed One teach the Dhamma! Let the Holy One teach the Dhamma!

20.6.pali: Santi sattā apparajakkhajātikā, assavanatā dhammassa parihāyanti.
20.6.english: There are beings with little dust in their eyes. They’re in decline because they haven’t heard the teaching.

20.7.english: There will be those who understand the teaching!’

20.8.english: That’s what the divinity Sahampati said.

20.9.english: Then he went on to say:

21.1.pali: Atha kho ahaṁ, bhikkhave, brahmuno ca ajjhesanaṁ viditvā sattesu ca kāruññataṁ paṭicca buddhacakkhunā lokaṁ volokesiṁ.
21.1.english: Then, understanding the Divinity’s invitation, I surveyed the world with the eye of a Buddha, because of my compassion for sentient beings.

21.2.pali: Addasaṁ kho ahaṁ, bhikkhave, buddhacakkhunā lokaṁ volokento satte apparajakkhe mahārajakkhe, tikkhindriye mudindriye, svākāre dvākāre, suviññāpaye duviññāpaye, appekacce paralokavajjabhayadassāvine viharante, appekacce na paralokavajjabhayadassāvine viharante.
21.2.english: And I saw sentient beings with little dust in their eyes, and some with much dust in their eyes; with keen faculties and with weak faculties, with good qualities and with bad qualities, easy to teach and hard to teach. And some of them lived seeing the danger in the fault to do with the next world, while others did not.

21.3.pali: Seyyathāpi nāma uppaliniyaṁ vā paduminiyaṁ vā puṇḍarīkiniyaṁ vā appekaccāni uppalāni vā padumāni vā puṇḍarīkāni vā udake jātāni udake saṁvaḍḍhāni udakānuggatāni antonimuggaposīni, appekaccāni uppalāni vā padumāni vā puṇḍarīkāni vā udake jātāni udake saṁvaḍḍhāni udakānuggatāni samodakaṁ ṭhitāni, appekaccāni uppalāni vā padumāni vā puṇḍarīkāni vā udake jātāni udake saṁvaḍḍhāni udakaṁ accuggamma ṭhitāni anupalittāni udakena;
21.3.english: It’s like a pool with blue water lilies, or pink or white lotuses. Some of them sprout and grow in the water without rising above it, thriving underwater. Some of them sprout and grow in the water reaching the water’s surface. And some of them sprout and grow in the water but rise up above the water and stand with no water clinging to them.

21.4.pali: evameva kho ahaṁ, bhikkhave, buddhacakkhunā lokaṁ volokento addasaṁ satte apparajakkhe mahārajakkhe, tikkhindriye mudindriye, svākāre dvākāre, suviññāpaye duviññāpaye, appekacce paralokavajjabhayadassāvine viharante, appekacce na paralokavajjabhayadassāvine viharante.
21.4.english: In the same way, I saw sentient beings with little dust in their eyes, and some with much dust in their eyes.

21.5.pali: Atha khvāhaṁ, bhikkhave, brahmānaṁ sahampatiṁ gāthāya paccabhāsiṁ:
21.5.english: Then I replied in verse to the divinity Sahampati:

21.6.english: ‘Flung open are the doors to freedom from death!

21.7.english: Let those with ears to hear commit to faith.

21.8.english: Thinking it would be troublesome, Divinity, <j>I did not teach

21.9.english: the sophisticated, sublime Dhamma among mankind.’

22.2.pali: ‘kassa nu kho ahaṁ paṭhamaṁ dhammaṁ deseyyaṁ;
22.2.english: ‘Who should I teach first of all?

22.3.english: Who will quickly understand this teaching?’

22.5.pali: ‘ayaṁ kho āḷāro kālāmo paṇḍito viyatto medhāvī dīgharattaṁ apparajakkhajātiko.
22.5.english: ‘That Āḷāra Kālāma is astute, competent, clever, and has long had little dust in his eyes.

22.6.pali: Yannūnāhaṁ āḷārassa kālāmassa paṭhamaṁ dhammaṁ deseyyaṁ.
22.6.english: Why don’t I teach him first of all?

22.8.pali: Atha kho maṁ, bhikkhave, devatā upasaṅkamitvā etadavoca:
22.8.english: But a deity came to me and said,

22.9.english: ‘Sir, Āḷāra Kālāma passed away seven days ago.’

23.2.pali: ‘kassa nu kho ahaṁ paṭhamaṁ dhammaṁ deseyyaṁ;
23.2.english: ‘Who should I teach first of all?

23.3.english: Who will quickly understand this teaching?’

23.5.pali: ‘ayaṁ kho udako rāmaputto paṇḍito viyatto medhāvī dīgharattaṁ apparajakkhajātiko.
23.5.english: ‘That Uddaka son of Rāma is astute, competent, clever, and has long had little dust in his eyes.

23.6.pali: Yannūnāhaṁ udakassa rāmaputtassa paṭhamaṁ dhammaṁ deseyyaṁ.
23.6.english: Why don’t I teach him first of all?

23.8.pali: Atha kho maṁ, bhikkhave, devatā upasaṅkamitvā etadavoca:
23.8.english: But a deity came to me and said,

23.9.english: ‘Sir, Uddaka son of Rāma passed away just last night.’

24.2.pali: ‘kassa nu kho ahaṁ paṭhamaṁ dhammaṁ deseyyaṁ;
24.2.english: ‘Who should I teach first of all?

24.3.english: Who will quickly understand this teaching?’

24.5.pali: ‘bahukārā kho me pañcavaggiyā bhikkhū, ye maṁ padhānapahitattaṁ upaṭṭhahiṁsu.
24.5.english: ‘The group of five mendicants were very helpful to me. They looked after me during my time of resolute striving.

24.6.pali: Yannūnāhaṁ pañcavaggiyānaṁ bhikkhūnaṁ paṭhamaṁ dhammaṁ deseyyan’ti.
24.6.english: Why don’t I teach them first of all?’

24.8.pali: ‘kahaṁ nu kho etarahi pañcavaggiyā bhikkhū viharantī’ti?
24.8.english: ‘Where are the group of five mendicants staying these days?’

24.9.pali: Addasaṁ kho ahaṁ, bhikkhave, dibbena cakkhunā visuddhena atikkantamānusakena pañcavaggiye bhikkhū bārāṇasiyaṁ viharante isipatane migadāye.
24.9.english: With clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman I saw that the group of five mendicants were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana.

25.1.pali: Addasā kho maṁ, bhikkhave, upako ājīvako antarā ca gayaṁ antarā ca bodhiṁ addhānamaggappaṭipannaṁ.
25.1.english: While I was traveling along the road between Gayā and Bodhgaya, the Ājīvaka ascetic Upaka saw me

25.3.pali: ‘vippasannāni kho te, āvuso, indriyāni, parisuddho chavivaṇṇo pariyodāto.
25.3.english: ‘Reverend, your faculties are so very clear, and your complexion is pure and bright.

25.4.pali: Kaṁsi tvaṁ, āvuso, uddissa pabbajito, ko vā te satthā, kassa vā tvaṁ dhammaṁ rocesī’ti?
25.4.english: In whose name have you gone forth, reverend? Who is your Teacher? Whose teaching do you believe in?’

25.5.pali: Evaṁ vutte, ahaṁ, bhikkhave, upakaṁ ājīvakaṁ gāthāhi ajjhabhāsiṁ:
25.5.english: I replied to Upaka in verse:

25.6.english: ‘I am the champion, the knower of all,

25.7.english: unsullied in the midst of all things.

25.8.english: I’ve given up all, freed through the ending of craving.

25.9.english: Since I know for myself, whose follower should I be?

26.1.pali: Atha khvāhaṁ, bhikkhave, anupubbena cārikaṁ caramāno yena bārāṇasī isipatanaṁ migadāyo yena pañcavaggiyā bhikkhū tenupasaṅkamiṁ.
26.1.english: Traveling stage by stage, I arrived at Varanasi, and went to see the group of five mendicants in the deer park at Isipatana.

26.2.pali: Addasaṁsu kho maṁ, bhikkhave, pañcavaggiyā bhikkhū dūrato āgacchantaṁ.
26.2.english: The group of five mendicants saw me coming off in the distance

26.4.pali: ‘ayaṁ kho, āvuso, samaṇo gotamo āgacchati bāhulliko padhānavibbhanto āvatto bāhullāya.
26.4.english: ‘Here comes the ascetic Gotama. He’s so indulgent; he strayed from the struggle and returned to indulgence.

26.5.pali: So neva abhivādetabbo, na paccuṭṭhātabbo; nāssa pattacīvaraṁ paṭiggahetabbaṁ.
26.5.english: We shouldn’t bow to him or rise for him or receive his bowl and robe.

26.6.pali: Api ca kho āsanaṁ ṭhapetabbaṁ, sace ākaṅkhissati nisīdissatī’ti.
26.6.english: But we can set out a seat; he can sit if he likes.’

26.7.pali: Yathā yathā kho ahaṁ, bhikkhave, upasaṅkamiṁ tathā tathā pañcavaggiyā bhikkhū nāsakkhiṁsu sakāya katikāya saṇṭhātuṁ.
26.7.english: Yet as I drew closer, the group of five mendicants were unable to stop themselves per their own agreement.

26.8.pali: Appekacce maṁ paccuggantvā pattacīvaraṁ paṭiggahesuṁ, appekacce āsanaṁ paññapesuṁ, appekacce pādodakaṁ upaṭṭhapesuṁ.
26.8.english: Some came out to greet me and receive my bowl and robe, some spread out a seat, while others set out water for washing my feet.

26.9.pali: Api ca kho maṁ nāmena ca āvusovādena ca samudācaranti.
26.9.english: But they still addressed me by name and as ‘reverend’.

27.1.pali: Evaṁ vutte, ahaṁ, bhikkhave, pañcavaggiye bhikkhū etadavocaṁ:

27.2.pali: ‘mā, bhikkhave, tathāgataṁ nāmena ca āvusovādena ca samudācaratha.
27.2.english: ‘Mendicants, don’t address me by name and as “reverend”.

27.3.english: The Realized One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha.

27.4.pali: Odahatha, bhikkhave, sotaṁ, amatamadhigataṁ, ahamanusāsāmi, ahaṁ dhammaṁ desemi.
27.4.english: Listen up, mendicants: I have achieved freedom from death! I shall instruct you, I will teach you the Dhamma.

27.5.pali: Yathānusiṭṭhaṁ tathā paṭipajjamānā nacirasseva—yassatthāya kulaputtā sammadeva agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajanti, tadanuttaraṁ—brahmacariyapariyosānaṁ diṭṭheva dhamme sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharissathā’ti.
27.5.english: By practicing as instructed you will soon realize the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. You will live having achieved with your own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.’

27.6.pali: Evaṁ vutte, bhikkhave, pañcavaggiyā bhikkhū maṁ etadavocuṁ:

27.7.pali: ‘tāyapi kho tvaṁ, āvuso gotama, iriyāya tāya paṭipadāya tāya dukkarakārikāya nājjhagamā uttari manussadhammā alamariyañāṇadassanavisesaṁ, kiṁ pana tvaṁ etarahi bāhulliko padhānavibbhanto āvatto bāhullāya adhigamissasi uttari manussadhammā alamariyañāṇadassanavisesan’ti?
27.7.english: ‘Reverend Gotama, even by that conduct, that practice, that grueling work you did not achieve any superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. How could you have achieved such a state now that you’ve become indulgent, strayed from the struggle and returned to indulgence?’

27.8.pali: Evaṁ vutte, ahaṁ, bhikkhave, pañcavaggiye bhikkhū etadavocaṁ:

27.9.pali: ‘na, bhikkhave, tathāgato bāhulliko, na padhānavibbhanto, na āvatto bāhullāya.
27.9.english: ‘The Realized One has not become indulgent, strayed from the struggle and returned to indulgence.

28.1.pali: Evaṁ vutte, ahaṁ, bhikkhave, pañcavaggiye bhikkhū etadavocaṁ:

28.2.pali: ‘abhijānātha me no tumhe, bhikkhave, ito pubbe evarūpaṁ pabhāvitametan’ti?
28.2.english: ‘Mendicants, have you ever known me to speak like this before?’

28.4.english: ‘The Realized One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha.

28.5.pali: Odahatha, bhikkhave, sotaṁ, amatamadhigataṁ, ahamanusāsāmi, ahaṁ dhammaṁ desemi.
28.5.english: Listen up, mendicants: I have achieved freedom from death! I shall instruct you, I will teach you the Dhamma.

28.6.pali: Yathānusiṭṭhaṁ tathā paṭipajjamānā nacirasseva—yassatthāya kulaputtā sammadeva agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajanti, tadanuttaraṁ—brahmacariyapariyosānaṁ diṭṭheva dhamme sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharissathā’ti.
28.6.english: By practicing as instructed you will soon realize the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. You will live having achieved with your own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.’

29.1.pali: Asakkhiṁ kho ahaṁ, bhikkhave, pañcavaggiye bhikkhū saññāpetuṁ.
29.1.english: I was able to persuade the group of five mendicants.

29.2.pali: Dvepi sudaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhū ovadāmi, tayo bhikkhū piṇḍāya caranti.
29.2.english: Then sometimes I advised two mendicants, while the other three went for alms.

29.3.pali: Yaṁ tayo bhikkhū piṇḍāya caritvā āharanti tena chabbaggiyā yāpema.
29.3.english: Then those three would feed all six of us with what they brought back.

29.4.pali: Tayopi sudaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhū ovadāmi, dve bhikkhū piṇḍāya caranti.
29.4.english: Sometimes I advised three mendicants, while the other two went for alms.

29.5.pali: Yaṁ dve bhikkhū piṇḍāya caritvā āharanti tena chabbaggiyā yāpema.
29.5.english: Then those two would feed all six of us with what they brought back.

3.10.pali: Atha kho āyasmā ānando bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
3.10.english: Then Ānanda said to the Buddha,

3.11.english: “Sir, the hermitage of the brahmin Rammaka is nearby.

3.14.pali: Sādhu, bhante, bhagavā yena rammakassa brāhmaṇassa assamo tenupasaṅkamatu anukampaṁ upādāyā”ti.
3.14.english: Please visit it out of sympathy.”

30.1.pali: Atha kho, bhikkhave, pañcavaggiyā bhikkhū mayā evaṁ ovadiyamānā evaṁ anusāsiyamānā attanā jātidhammā samānā jātidhamme ādīnavaṁ viditvā ajātaṁ anuttaraṁ yogakkhemaṁ nibbānaṁ pariyesamānā ajātaṁ anuttaraṁ yogakkhemaṁ nibbānaṁ ajjhagamaṁsu, attanā jarādhammā samānā jarādhamme ādīnavaṁ viditvā ajaraṁ anuttaraṁ yogakkhemaṁ nibbānaṁ pariyesamānā ajaraṁ anuttaraṁ yogakkhemaṁ nibbānaṁ ajjhagamaṁsu, attanā byādhidhammā samānā …pe… attanā maraṇadhammā samānā … attanā sokadhammā samānā … attanā saṅkilesadhammā samānā saṅkilesadhamme ādīnavaṁ viditvā asaṅkiliṭṭhaṁ anuttaraṁ yogakkhemaṁ nibbānaṁ pariyesamānā asaṅkiliṭṭhaṁ anuttaraṁ yogakkhemaṁ nibbānaṁ ajjhagamaṁsu.
30.1.english: As the group of five mendicants were being advised and instructed by me like this, being themselves liable to be reborn, understanding the drawbacks in being liable to be reborn, they sought that which is free of rebirth, the supreme sanctuary from the yoke, extinguishment—and they found it. Being themselves liable to grow old, fall sick, die, sorrow, and become corrupted, understanding the drawbacks in these things, they sought that which is free of old age, sickness, death, sorrow, and corruption, the supreme sanctuary from the yoke, extinguishment—and they found it.

30.2.english: Knowledge and vision arose in them:

30.3.pali: ‘akuppā no vimutti, ayamantimā jāti, natthi dāni punabbhavo’ti.
30.3.english: ‘Our freedom is unshakable; this is our last rebirth; now there’ll be no more future lives.’

31.1.english: Mendicants, there are these five kinds of sensual stimulation.

31.3.pali: Cakkhuviññeyyā rūpā iṭṭhā kantā manāpā piyarūpā kāmūpasaṁhitā rajanīyā,
31.3.english: Sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing.

31.7.pali: kāyaviññeyyā phoṭṭhabbā iṭṭhā kantā manāpā piyarūpā kāmūpasaṁhitā rajanīyā.
31.7.english: Touches known by the body, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing.

31.8.english: These are the five kinds of sensual stimulation.

32.1.pali: Ye hi keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā ime pañca kāmaguṇe gathitā mucchitā ajjhopannā anādīnavadassāvino anissaraṇapaññā paribhuñjanti, te evamassu veditabbā:
32.1.english: There are ascetics and brahmins who enjoy these five kinds of sensual stimulation tied, infatuated, attached, blind to the drawbacks, and not understanding the escape. You should understand that they

32.2.english: have met with calamity and disaster, and the Wicked One can do with them what he wants.

32.3.pali: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, āraññako mago baddho pāsarāsiṁ adhisayeyya.
32.3.english: Suppose a deer in the wilderness was lying caught on a pile of snares.

32.5.english: has met with calamity and disaster, and the hunter can do with them what he wants.

32.6.pali: Āgacchante ca pana ludde yena kāmaṁ na pakkamissatī’ti.
32.6.english: And when the hunter comes, it cannot flee where it wants.

32.7.pali: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, ye hi keci samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā ime pañca kāmaguṇe gathitā mucchitā ajjhopannā anādīnavadassāvino anissaraṇapaññā paribhuñjanti, te evamassu veditabbā:
32.7.english: In the same way, there are ascetics and brahmins who enjoy these five kinds of sensual stimulation tied, infatuated, attached, blind to the drawbacks, and not understanding the escape. You should understand that they

32.8.english: have met with calamity and disaster, and the Wicked One can do with them what he wants.

33.1.pali: Ye ca kho keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā ime pañca kāmaguṇe agathitā amucchitā anajjhopannā ādīnavadassāvino nissaraṇapaññā paribhuñjanti, te evamassu veditabbā:
33.1.english: There are ascetics and brahmins who enjoy these five kinds of sensual stimulation without being tied, infatuated, or attached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape. You should understand that they

33.2.pali: ‘na anayamāpannā na byasanamāpannā na yathākāmakaraṇīyā pāpimato’.
33.2.english: haven’t met with calamity and disaster, and the Wicked One cannot do what he wants with them.

33.3.pali: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, āraññako mago abaddho pāsarāsiṁ adhisayeyya.
33.3.english: Suppose a deer in the wilderness was lying on a pile of snares without being caught.

33.5.pali: ‘na anayamāpanno na byasanamāpanno na yathākāmakaraṇīyo luddassa.
33.5.english: hasn’t met with calamity and disaster, and the hunter cannot do what he wants with them.

33.6.pali: Āgacchante ca pana ludde yena kāmaṁ pakkamissatī’ti.
33.6.english: And when the hunter comes, it can flee where it wants.

33.7.pali: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, ye hi keci samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā ime pañca kāmaguṇe agathitā amucchitā anajjhopannā ādīnavadassāvino nissaraṇapaññā paribhuñjanti, te evamassu veditabbā:
33.7.english: In the same way, there are ascetics and brahmins who enjoy these five kinds of sensual stimulation without being tied, infatuated, or attached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape. You should understand that they

33.8.pali: ‘na anayamāpannā na byasanamāpannā na yathākāmakaraṇīyā pāpimato’.
33.8.english: haven’t met with calamity and disaster, and the Wicked One cannot do what he wants with them.

34.1.pali: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, āraññako mago araññe pavane caramāno vissattho gacchati, vissattho tiṭṭhati, vissattho nisīdati, vissattho seyyaṁ kappeti.
34.1.english: Suppose there was a wild deer wandering in the forest that walked, stood, sat, and laid down in confidence.

34.3.english: Because it’s out of the hunter’s range.

34.4.pali: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu vivicceva kāmehi vivicca akusalehi dhammehi savitakkaṁ savicāraṁ vivekajaṁ pītisukhaṁ paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
34.4.english: In the same way, a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected.

34.5.pali: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu andhamakāsi māraṁ apadaṁ, vadhitvā māracakkhuṁ adassanaṁ gato pāpimato.
34.5.english: This is called a mendicant who has blinded Māra, put out his eyes without a trace, and gone where the Wicked One cannot see.

35.1.pali: Puna caparaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā ajjhattaṁ sampasādanaṁ cetaso ekodibhāvaṁ avitakkaṁ avicāraṁ samādhijaṁ pītisukhaṁ dutiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
35.1.english: Furthermore, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, a mendicant enters and remains in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected.

35.2.english: This is called a mendicant who has blinded Māra …

36.1.pali: Puna caparaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu pītiyā ca virāgā upekkhako ca viharati, sato ca sampajāno, sukhañca kāyena paṭisaṁvedeti yaṁ taṁ ariyā ācikkhanti ‘upekkhako satimā sukhavihārī’ti tatiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
36.1.english: Furthermore, with the fading away of rapture, a mendicant enters and remains in the third absorption, where they meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’

36.2.english: This is called a mendicant who has blinded Māra …

37.1.pali: Puna caparaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sukhassa ca pahānā dukkhassa ca pahānā pubbeva somanassadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamā adukkhamasukhaṁ upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ catutthaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
37.1.english: Furthermore, with the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, a mendicant enters and remains in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness.

37.2.english: This is called a mendicant who has blinded Māra …

38.1.pali: Puna caparaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso rūpasaññānaṁ samatikkamā paṭighasaññānaṁ atthaṅgamā nānattasaññānaṁ amanasikārā ‘ananto ākāso’ti ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
38.1.english: Furthermore, a mendicant, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space.

38.2.english: This is called a mendicant who has blinded Māra …

39.1.pali: Puna caparaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma ‘anantaṁ viññāṇan’ti viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
39.1.english: Furthermore, a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite consciousness.

39.2.english: This is called a mendicant who has blinded Māra …

4.11.pali: Etaṁ kho, bhikkhave, tumhākaṁ patirūpaṁ kulaputtānaṁ saddhā agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajitānaṁ yaṁ tumhe dhammiyā kathāya sannisīdeyyātha.
4.11.english: It’s appropriate for gentlemen like you, who have gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness, to sit together and talk about the teaching.

4.12.english: When you’re sitting together you should do one of two things:

4.13.pali: dhammī vā kathā, ariyo vā tuṇhībhāvo.
4.13.english: discuss the teachings or keep noble silence.

40.1.pali: Puna caparaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma ‘natthi kiñcī’ti ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
40.1.english: Furthermore, a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness.

40.2.english: This is called a mendicant who has blinded Māra …

41.1.pali: Puna caparaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ samatikkamma nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
41.1.english: Furthermore, a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, enters and remains in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.

41.2.english: This is called a mendicant who has blinded Māra …

42.1.pali: Puna caparaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ samatikkamma saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ upasampajja viharati, paññāya cassa disvā āsavā parikkhīṇā honti.
42.1.english: Furthermore, a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end.

42.2.pali: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu andhamakāsi māraṁ apadaṁ, vadhitvā māracakkhuṁ adassanaṁ gato pāpimato.
42.2.english: This is called a mendicant who has blinded Māra, put out his eyes without a trace, and gone where the Wicked One cannot see.

42.3.pali: Tiṇṇo loke visattikaṁ vissattho gacchati, vissattho tiṭṭhati, vissattho nisīdati, vissattho seyyaṁ kappeti.
42.3.english: They’ve crossed over clinging to the world. And they walk, stand, sit, and lie down in confidence.

42.5.english: Because they’re out of the Wicked One’s range.”

42.6.english: That is what the Buddha said.

42.7.pali: Attamanā te bhikkhū bhagavato bhāsitaṁ abhinandunti.
42.7.english: Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said.

15.10.pali: addhā āḷāro kālāmo imaṁ dhammaṁ jānaṁ passaṁ viharatī’ti.
15.10.english: Surely he meditates knowing and seeing this teaching.’

15.11.pali: Atha khvāhaṁ, bhikkhave, yena āḷāro kālāmo tenupasaṅkamiṁ; upasaṅkamitvā āḷāraṁ kālāmaṁ etadavocaṁ:
15.11.english: So I approached Āḷāra Kālāma and said to him,

15.12.pali: ‘kittāvatā no, āvuso kālāma, imaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharāmīti pavedesī’ti?
15.12.english: ‘Reverend Kālāma, to what extent do you say you’ve realized this teaching with your own insight?’

15.13.pali: Evaṁ vutte, bhikkhave, āḷāro kālāmo ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ pavedesi.
15.13.english: When I said this, he declared the dimension of nothingness.

15.15.pali: ‘na kho āḷārasseva kālāmassa atthi saddhā, mayhampatthi saddhā;
15.15.english: ‘It’s not just Āḷāra Kālāma who has faith,

15.16.pali: na kho āḷārasseva kālāmassa atthi vīriyaṁ, mayhampatthi vīriyaṁ;

15.17.pali: na kho āḷārasseva kālāmassa atthi sati, mayhampatthi sati;

15.18.pali: na kho āḷārasseva kālāmassa atthi samādhi, mayhampatthi samādhi;

15.19.pali: na kho āḷārasseva kālāmassa atthi paññā, mayhampatthi paññā.
15.19.english: and wisdom; I too have these things.

15.20.pali: Yannūnāhaṁ yaṁ dhammaṁ āḷāro kālāmo sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharāmīti pavedeti, tassa dhammassa sacchikiriyāya padaheyyan’ti.
15.20.english: Why don’t I make an effort to realize the same teaching that Āḷāra Kālāma says he has realized with his own insight?’

15.21.pali: So kho ahaṁ, bhikkhave, nacirasseva khippameva taṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja vihāsiṁ.
15.21.english: I quickly realized that teaching with my own insight, and lived having achieved it.

15.22.pali: Atha khvāhaṁ, bhikkhave, yena āḷāro kālāmo tenupasaṅkamiṁ; upasaṅkamitvā āḷāraṁ kālāmaṁ etadavocaṁ:
15.22.english: So I approached Āḷāra Kālāma and said to him,

15.23.pali: ‘Ettāvatā no, āvuso kālāma, imaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja pavedesī’ti?
15.23.english: ‘Reverend Kālāma, is it up to this point that you realized this teaching with your own insight, and declare having achieved it?’

15.24.pali: ‘Ettāvatā kho ahaṁ, āvuso, imaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja pavedemī’ti.

15.25.pali: ‘Ahampi kho, āvuso, ettāvatā imaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharāmī’ti.
15.25.english: ‘I too, reverend, have realized this teaching with my own insight up to this point, and live having achieved it.’

15.26.english: ‘We are fortunate, reverend, so very fortunate

15.27.pali: ye mayaṁ āyasmantaṁ tādisaṁ sabrahmacāriṁ passāma.
15.27.english: to see a venerable such as yourself as one of our spiritual companions!

15.28.pali: Iti yāhaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja pavedemi taṁ tvaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharasi.
15.28.english: So the teaching that I’ve realized with my own insight, and declare having achieved it, you’ve realized with your own insight, and dwell having achieved it.

15.29.pali: Yaṁ tvaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharasi tamahaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja pavedemi.
15.29.english: The teaching that you’ve realized with your own insight, and dwell having achieved it, I’ve realized with my own insight, and declare having achieved it.

15.30.pali: Iti yāhaṁ dhammaṁ jānāmi taṁ tvaṁ dhammaṁ jānāsi, yaṁ tvaṁ dhammaṁ jānāsi tamahaṁ dhammaṁ jānāmi.
15.30.english: So the teaching that I know, you know, and the teaching that you know, I know.

15.31.pali: Iti yādiso ahaṁ tādiso tuvaṁ, yādiso tuvaṁ tādiso ahaṁ.
15.31.english: I am like you and you are like me.

15.32.pali: Ehi dāni, āvuso, ubhova santā imaṁ gaṇaṁ pariharāmā’ti.
15.32.english: Come now, reverend! We should both lead this community together.’

15.33.pali: Iti kho, bhikkhave, āḷāro kālāmo ācariyo me samāno attano antevāsiṁ maṁ samānaṁ attanā samasamaṁ ṭhapesi, uḷārāya ca maṁ pūjāya pūjesi.
15.33.english: And that is how my tutor Āḷāra Kālāma placed me, his pupil, on the same position as him, and honored me with high praise.

15.35.pali: ‘nāyaṁ dhammo nibbidāya na virāgāya na nirodhāya na upasamāya na abhiññāya na sambodhāya na nibbānāya saṁvattati, yāvadeva ākiñcaññāyatanūpapattiyā’ti.
15.35.english: ‘This teaching doesn’t lead to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. It only leads as far as rebirth in the dimension of nothingness.’

15.36.pali: So kho ahaṁ, bhikkhave, taṁ dhammaṁ analaṅkaritvā tasmā dhammā nibbijja apakkamiṁ.
15.36.english: Realizing that this teaching was inadequate, I left disappointed.

16.10.pali: addhā rāmo imaṁ dhammaṁ jānaṁ passaṁ vihāsī’ti.
16.10.english: Surely he meditated knowing and seeing this teaching.’

16.11.pali: Atha khvāhaṁ, bhikkhave, yena udako rāmaputto tenupasaṅkamiṁ; upasaṅkamitvā udakaṁ rāmaputtaṁ etadavocaṁ:
16.11.english: So I approached Uddaka son of Rāma and said to him,

16.12.pali: ‘kittāvatā no, āvuso, rāmo imaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharāmīti pavedesī’ti?
16.12.english: ‘Reverend, to what extent did Rāma say he’d realized this teaching with his own insight?’

16.13.pali: Evaṁ vutte, bhikkhave, udako rāmaputto nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ pavedesi.
16.13.english: When I said this, Uddaka son of Rāma declared the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.

16.15.pali: ‘na kho rāmasseva ahosi saddhā, mayhampatthi saddhā;
16.15.english: ‘It’s not just Rāma who had faith,

16.16.pali: na kho rāmasseva ahosi vīriyaṁ, mayhampatthi vīriyaṁ;

16.17.pali: na kho rāmasseva ahosi sati, mayhampatthi sati;

16.18.pali: na kho rāmasseva ahosi samādhi, mayhampatthi samādhi,

16.19.pali: na kho rāmasseva ahosi paññā, mayhampatthi paññā.
16.19.english: and wisdom; I too have these things.

16.20.pali: Yannūnāhaṁ yaṁ dhammaṁ rāmo sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharāmīti pavedesi, tassa dhammassa sacchikiriyāya padaheyyan’ti.
16.20.english: Why don’t I make an effort to realize the same teaching that Rāma said he had realized with his own insight?’

16.21.pali: So kho ahaṁ, bhikkhave, nacirasseva khippameva taṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja vihāsiṁ.
16.21.english: I quickly realized that teaching with my own insight, and lived having achieved it.

16.22.pali: Atha khvāhaṁ, bhikkhave, yena udako rāmaputto tenupasaṅkamiṁ; upasaṅkamitvā udakaṁ rāmaputtaṁ etadavocaṁ:
16.22.english: So I approached Uddaka son of Rāma and said to him,

16.23.pali: ‘Ettāvatā no, āvuso, rāmo imaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja pavedesī’ti?
16.23.english: ‘Reverend, had Rāma realized this teaching with his own insight up to this point, and declared having achieved it?’

16.24.pali: ‘Ettāvatā kho, āvuso, rāmo imaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja pavedesī’ti.

16.25.pali: ‘Ahampi kho, āvuso, ettāvatā imaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharāmī’ti.
16.25.english: ‘I too have realized this teaching with my own insight up to this point, and live having achieved it.’

16.26.english: ‘We are fortunate, reverend, so very fortunate

16.27.pali: ye mayaṁ āyasmantaṁ tādisaṁ sabrahmacāriṁ passāma.
16.27.english: to see a venerable such as yourself as one of our spiritual companions!

16.28.pali: Iti yaṁ dhammaṁ rāmo sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja pavedesi, taṁ tvaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharasi.
16.28.english: So the teaching that Rāma had realized with his own insight, and declared having achieved it, you’ve realized with your own insight, and dwell having achieved it.

16.29.pali: Yaṁ tvaṁ dhammaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharasi, taṁ dhammaṁ rāmo sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja pavedesi.
16.29.english: The teaching that you’ve realized with your own insight, and dwell having achieved it, Rāma had realized with his own insight, and declared having achieved it.

16.30.pali: Iti yaṁ dhammaṁ rāmo abhiññāsi taṁ tvaṁ dhammaṁ jānāsi, yaṁ tvaṁ dhammaṁ jānāsi, taṁ dhammaṁ rāmo abhiññāsi.
16.30.english: So the teaching that Rāma directly knew, you know, and the teaching you know, Rāma directly knew.

16.31.pali: Iti yādiso rāmo ahosi tādiso tuvaṁ, yādiso tuvaṁ tādiso rāmo ahosi.
16.31.english: Rāma was like you and you are like Rāma.

16.32.pali: Ehi dāni, āvuso, tuvaṁ imaṁ gaṇaṁ pariharā’ti.
16.32.english: Come now, reverend! You should lead this community.’

16.33.pali: Iti kho, bhikkhave, udako rāmaputto sabrahmacārī me samāno ācariyaṭṭhāne maṁ ṭhapesi, uḷārāya ca maṁ pūjāya pūjesi.
16.33.english: And that is how my spiritual companion Uddaka son of Rāma placed me in the position of a tutor and honored me with high praise.

16.35.pali: ‘nāyaṁ dhammo nibbidāya na virāgāya na nirodhāya na upasamāya na abhiññāya na sambodhāya na nibbānāya saṁvattati, yāvadeva nevasaññānāsaññāyatanūpapattiyā’ti.
16.35.english: ‘This teaching doesn’t lead to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. It only leads as far as rebirth in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.’

16.36.pali: So kho ahaṁ, bhikkhave, taṁ dhammaṁ analaṅkaritvā tasmā dhammā nibbijja apakkamiṁ.
16.36.english: Realizing that this teaching was inadequate, I left disappointed.

19.10.english: Those mired in greed and hate

19.12.english: It goes against the stream, subtle,

19.14.english: Those besotted by greed cannot see,

19.15.english: for they’re shrouded in a mass of darkness.’

19.16.pali: Itiha me, bhikkhave, paṭisañcikkhato appossukkatāya cittaṁ namati, no dhammadesanāya.
19.16.english: So, as I reflected like this, my mind inclined to remaining passive, not to teaching the Dhamma.

20.10.english: ‘Among the Magadhans there appeared in the past

20.11.english: an impure teaching thought up by the stained.

20.12.english: Fling open the door to freedom from death!

20.13.english: Let them hear the teaching <j>the immaculate one discovered.

20.14.english: Standing high on a rocky mountain,

20.15.english: you can see the people all around.

20.16.english: In just the same way, All-seer, so intelligent,

20.17.english: having ascended the Temple of Truth,

20.18.english: rid of sorrow, look upon the people

20.19.english: swamped with sorrow, overcome by rebirth and old age.

20.20.english: Rise, hero! Victor in battle, caravan leader,

20.21.english: wander the world cleared of debt.

20.22.english: Let the Blessed One teach the Dhamma!

20.23.english: There will be those who understand!’

21.10.pali: Atha kho, bhikkhave, brahmā sahampati ‘katāvakāso khomhi bhagavatā dhammadesanāyā’ti maṁ abhivādetvā padakkhiṇaṁ katvā tatthevantaradhāyi.
21.10.english: Then the divinity Sahampati, knowing that his request for me to teach the Dhamma had been granted, bowed and respectfully circled me, keeping me on his right, before vanishing right there.

22.10.english: And knowledge and vision arose in me,

22.11.english: ‘Āḷāra Kālāma passed away seven days ago.’

22.13.english: ‘This is a great loss for Āḷāra Kālāma.

22.14.pali: Sace hi so imaṁ dhammaṁ suṇeyya, khippameva ājāneyyā’ti.
22.14.english: If he had heard the teaching, he would have understood it quickly.’

23.10.english: And knowledge and vision arose in me,

23.11.english: ‘Uddaka son of Rāma passed away just last night.’

23.13.english: ‘This is a great loss for Uddaka.

23.14.pali: Sace hi so imaṁ dhammaṁ suṇeyya, khippameva ājāneyyā’ti.
23.14.english: If he had heard the teaching, he would have understood it quickly.’

24.10.pali: Atha khvāhaṁ, bhikkhave, uruvelāyaṁ yathābhirantaṁ viharitvā yena bārāṇasī tena cārikaṁ pakkamiṁ.
24.10.english: So, when I had stayed in Uruvelā as long as I pleased, I set out for Varanasi.

25.12.english: In the world with its gods,

25.14.english: For in this world, I am the perfected one;

25.18.english: I am going to the city of Kāsi

25.19.english: to roll forth the Wheel of Dhamma.

25.20.english: In this world that is so blind,

25.21.english: I’ll beat the drum of freedom from death!’

25.22.pali: ‘Yathā kho tvaṁ, āvuso, paṭijānāsi, arahasi anantajino’ti.
25.22.english: ‘According to what you claim, reverend, you ought to be the Infinite Victor.’

25.23.english: ‘The victors are those who, like me,

25.24.english: have reached the ending of defilements.

25.25.english: I have conquered bad qualities, Upaka—

25.27.pali: Evaṁ vutte, bhikkhave, upako ājīvako ‘hupeyyapāvuso’ti vatvā sīsaṁ okampetvā ummaggaṁ gahetvā pakkāmi.
25.27.english: When I had spoken, Upaka said: ‘If you say so, reverend.’ Shaking his head, he took a wrong turn and left.

27.10.english: The Realized One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha.

27.11.pali: Odahatha, bhikkhave, sotaṁ, amatamadhigataṁ, ahamanusāsāmi, ahaṁ dhammaṁ desemi.
27.11.english: Listen up, mendicants: I have achieved freedom from death! I shall instruct you, I will teach you the Dhamma.

27.12.pali: Yathānusiṭṭhaṁ tathā paṭipajjamānā nacirasseva—yassatthāya kulaputtā sammadeva agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajanti, tadanuttaraṁ—brahmacariyapariyosānaṁ diṭṭheva dhamme sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharissathā’ti.
27.12.english: By practicing as instructed you will soon realize the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life.’

27.13.pali: Dutiyampi kho, bhikkhave, pañcavaggiyā bhikkhū maṁ etadavocuṁ:
27.13.english: But for a second time they said to me,

27.14.pali: ‘tāyapi kho tvaṁ, āvuso gotama, iriyāya tāya paṭipadāya tāya dukkarakārikāya nājjhagamā uttari manussadhammā alamariyañāṇadassanavisesaṁ, kiṁ pana tvaṁ etarahi bāhulliko padhānavibbhanto āvatto bāhullāya adhigamissasi uttari manussadhammā alamariyañāṇadassanavisesan’ti?

27.15.pali: Dutiyampi kho ahaṁ, bhikkhave, pañcavaggiye bhikkhū etadavocaṁ:
27.15.english: So for a second time I said to them,

27.16.english: ‘The Realized One has not become indulgent …’

27.18.pali: Tatiyampi kho, bhikkhave, pañcavaggiyā bhikkhū maṁ etadavocuṁ:
27.18.english: But for a third time they said to me,

27.19.pali: ‘tāyapi kho tvaṁ, āvuso gotama, iriyāya tāya paṭipadāya tāya dukkarakārikāya nājjhagamā uttari manussadhammā alamariyañāṇadassanavisesaṁ, kiṁ pana tvaṁ etarahi bāhulliko padhānavibbhanto āvatto bāhullāya adhigamissasi uttari manussadhammā alamariyañāṇadassanavisesan’ti?
27.19.english: ‘Reverend Gotama, even by that conduct, that practice, that grueling work you did not achieve any superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. How could you have achieved such a state now that you’ve become indulgent, strayed from the struggle and returned to indulgence?’
